Name: spectral_quantization_routing
Title: Spectral Quantization Routing: Training-Free Precision Allocation via Activation Covariance Geometry
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states serve as direct proxies for quantization sensitivity, enabling training-free precision allocation via activation covariance geometry.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent advances in spectral latent steering and geometric interference cancellation, but differs in its focus on training-free precision allocation and its use of activation covariance geometry as a proxy for quantization sensitivity. Unlike existing methods, our approach replaces qualitative fragility claims with formalized risk bounds, providing a transparent and reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference promises significant latency and memory gains, but typically relies on static profiling or costly calibration. We propose Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry. SQR computes local covariance condition numbers and spectral decay rates of hidden states, mapping them to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground the routing mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds, linking spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, SQR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Experiments: Evaluate SQR on WILDS benchmarks, comparing its performance to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Investigate the effect of different precision-switching policies on SQR's performance and latency.
Apply SQR to various deep learning models and tasks, assessing its generalizability and versatility.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The accuracy of SQR's precision allocation may be affected by the quality of the activation covariance estimates.
The computational overhead of SQR's precision-switching policy may impact its suitability for real-time applications.
The generalizability of SQR to different models and tasks may be limited by its reliance on specific architectural assumptions.
